variables.φ: Phase difference between voltage and current (rad)

locked_facts: AC voltage: V = V₀ sin(ωt), alternates polarity with period T = 1/f = 2π/ω
AC current: I = I₀ sin(ωt + φ), where φ is the phase difference relative to voltage
RMS voltage: V_rms = V₀/√2 ≈ 0.707 V₀ (the DC equivalent for heating effect)
Average power: P_avg = V_rms × I_rms × cos(φ) — only resistive component dissipates power
Indian mains: V_rms = 220 V, so V₀ = 220√2 ≈ 311 V, f = 50 Hz
Average value of AC over full cycle = 0; average of |V| over half cycle = 2V₀/π
truth_statements: The RMS value of AC is the equivalent DC value that produces the same heating effect in a resistor
When we say household supply is 220 V, it refers to V_rms — the peak voltage is actually 311 V
The average value of a sinusoidal AC over a complete cycle is zero because positive and negative halves cancel
Phase difference φ depends on the circuit: φ = 0 for pure R, +π/2 for pure L, -π/2 for pure C
Average power dissipated depends on cos(φ) — in pure L or pure C circuits, average power is zero (wattless current)

aha_moment.what_to_show: A sinusoidal V(t) waveform with V₀ = 311 V. The V_rms = 220 V line drawn horizontally. Shaded area showing that the squared waveform (V²) has average equal to V₀²/2 — so V_rms = V₀/√2. A DC source at 220 V heats a resistor identically to this AC source.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: V² waveform plotted. Its average (V₀²/2) shown as horizontal line. √(average of V²) = V₀/√2 = V_rms. Side by side: AC bulb and DC bulb at same brightness.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see WHY V_rms = V₀/√2 — it comes from averaging V², not V. The √2 is not arbitrary.

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: Sinusoidal V(t) = V₀ sin(ωt) waveform plotted. Peak value V₀, time period T, frequency f labeled. V₀ = 311 V marked. Zero crossings and peaks identified.
simulation_states.emphasis: AC voltage alternates between +V₀ and -V₀ with period T = 1/f

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: V_rms line drawn at V₀/√2 = 220 V. The V² waveform plotted below — always positive, oscillates between 0 and V₀². Average of V² shown as V₀²/2. Connection: V_rms = √(V₀²/2) = V₀/√2.
simulation_states.emphasis: RMS means root of MEAN of SQUARE — first square V, then average, then take square root

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: Side by side: AC source (V_rms = 220 V) and DC source (V = 220 V) connected to identical resistors. Both produce same heating (same brightness bulb). Power comparison: P_AC_avg = V²_rms/R = V₀²/(2R) = P_DC. The peak voltage 311 V is higher than 220 V — the meter reads 220 V rms.
simulation_states.emphasis: 220 V on your wall socket is V_rms. The actual peak is 311 V. RMS is the effective DC equivalent.

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: adjust V₀, frequency, and phase angle φ. Plot V(t) and I(t) together. See V_rms and I_rms update. Calculate average power P = V_rms I_rms cos(φ). Change φ to see power drop to zero at φ = 90°.
simulation_states.emphasis: At φ = 0: maximum power. At φ = 90°: zero average power (wattless current). Indian mains: 220 V, 50 Hz.

common_misconceptions.belief: 220 V AC means the voltage is always 220 V
common_misconceptions.correction: 220 V is the RMS value. The actual voltage oscillates between +311 V and -311 V (since V₀ = 220√2 ≈ 311 V). At any instant, V can be anywhere from -311 to +311 V. The value 220 V is the effective DC equivalent for heating.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show sinusoid oscillating between ±311 V with the 220 V rms line — voltage is NOT constant

common_misconceptions.correction: The AVERAGE value of AC voltage over a full cycle is ZERO (positive and negative halves cancel). V₀/√2 is the RMS value, not the average. The average of the absolute value over half a cycle is 2V₀/π ≈ 0.637V₀, which is different from V_rms = 0.707V₀.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show full cycle: positive area + negative area = 0 average. Then show V² always positive → its average is V₀²/2 → RMS = V₀/√2

legend.meaning: Instantaneous voltage as function of time
Phase difference between V and I
